title: The faces of excellence, innovation and inspiration
description: Dr. Lahari Surapaneni: Advancing Excellence in Aesthetic Surgery and Preventive Healthcare LeadershipDr. Lahari Surapaneni is a distinguished plastic and reconstructive surgeon, entrepreneur, and healthcare leader redefining aesthetic and reconstructive surgery standards in India. Serving as Director of Plastic, Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery and CEO of Bangalore Hospitals Group, she combines advanced surgical expertise with patient-centered healthcare leadership. Her qualifications include MBBS, MS in General Surgery, MCh in Plastic Surgery, and ECFMG certified, MHA from the USC, placing her among a rare group of globally trained surgeons practicing in India. Recognized for techniques such as Inverse Hybrid Rhinoplasty and scarless endoscopic facelifts, she is known for delivering natural, anatomically balanced outcomes. Beyond surgery, a pioneer in precision medicine, she is building programmes that bring structured, preventive healthcare to patients long before they ever need a surgeon.Mr. Kishor Jagirdar : Transforming Businesses Through Strategic Innovation and LeadershipMr. Kishor Jagirdar  is a visionary business leader and transformation strategist associated with Infopace Management Pvt. Ltd., a Bengaluru-based strategic change management and business transformation firm established in 1999. Under his leadership, Infopace has assisted organizations across sectors including IT, telecom, retail, manufacturing, and services achieve sustainable growth, operational excellence, and digital maturity. The company specializes in Growth Acceleration   Programs, Digital Transformation, Global Capability Center (GCC) establishment, leadership development, and strategic investments. By effectively aligning people, processes, technology, and business objectives, he has enabled enterprises to navigate complex market challenges and accelerate innovation. His commitment to organizational excellence, strategic execution, and future-ready business solutions has positioned Infopace as a trusted partner for companies seeking long-term success, enhanced competitiveness, and transformational growth in an increasingly dynamic global business environment.Mr. Ranjeett Sahu: Driving Industrial Growth Through Visionary Entrepreneurship and Diversified LeadershipRanjeett Sahu is a dynamic entrepreneur and business leader recognized for building and scaling successful ventures across steel, metallurgy, industrial gases, hospitality and luxury jewellery. Alongside a forward-thinking group management team, he has played a pivotal role in driving the growth of RS Virla Group and KRN Alloys Pvt Ltd through a shared vision focused on innovation, operational excellence, and long-term value creation. The group’s management philosophy is built on disciplined execution, technology adoption, customer-centricity, and sustainable growth, enabling businesses within the group to remain competitive in evolving markets. With deep expertise in the steel and metals industry, the leadership team has consistently emphasized modernization, process-driven manufacturing, and efficiency-led expansion. Beyond manufacturing, the group’s successful diversification into hospitality, industrial gas solutions, and bespoke jewelry reflects its entrepreneurial mindset, strategic adaptability, and strong business foresight. Known for collaborative leadership and progressive thinking, Ranjeett Sahu and the group management continue to foster innovation, strengthen enterprises, and contribute meaningfully to the future of Indian industry and business.https://www.rsvirla.comVijay Kumar Amar: A Lifetime of Excellence in Infrastructure Engineering and Public ServiceVijay Kumar Amar is a distinguished civil and infrastructure engineering professional with over five decades of experience in highways, bridges, urban development, arbitration, and sustainable infrastructure management. Holding qualifications including B.E., M.Tech. (India), PG Diploma (USA), PG Diploma (UK), MBA, LL.B., and an Honorary Doctorate, he has led major projects funded by the World Bank, ADB, HUDCO, and NHAI. As Chief Engineer of the Madhya Pradesh Public Works Department, he supervised over 4,000 kilometers of national highways and contributed significantly to Bhopal’s urban modernization. He has also served as an arbitrator and dispute resolution expert for infrastructure projects across India. His outstanding contributions earned him Gold Medals from both the President of India and the President of Sri Lanka, recognizing his technical excellence, leadership, and commitment to sustainable infrastructure development.Aripirala Yogananda Sastry: A Young Prodigy in Astrology and VastuAripirala Yogananda Sastry, a 13-year-old prodigy from Guntur, Andhra Pradesh, has distinguished himself as an extraordinary talent in the fields of Astrology and Vastu. Recognized among the youngest achievers to receive a doctorate in these disciplines, he has gained national and international recognition for his exceptional understanding of Vedic sciences. His remarkable journey has been celebrated by multiple record organizations, and he has earned over 275 prestigious awards and honors. His achievements have received appreciation from Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Defence Minister Rajnath Singh, while he has also been felicitated by governors, ministers, judges, spiritual leaders, and eminent personalities. Through his dedication, discipline, and passion for preserving India’s ancient wisdom, Yogananda Sastry continues to inspire the younger generation.Mr. Nitin Kumar: Driving Enterprise AI from Innovation to Scalable Business ImpactNitin Kumar, Chief Technology Officer at LiftLab, is among the emerging enterprise AI leaders shaping how businesses operationalize intelligence at scale. With over 18 years of experience across consulting, product innovation, and enterprise technology, he has built a reputation for transforming AI from experimental models into systems that drive measurable business decisions. At LiftLab, he leads the architecture and engineering strategy behind a next-generation decision intelligence platform designed for real-world enterprise deployment. Previously, he scaled C5i’s AI practice into a global team of researchers and engineers, built deep learning capabilities at EY for banking and risk systems, and conceptualized the AI product that evolved into Incivus. His work sits at the intersection of AI architecture, operational strategy, and scalable enterprise execution.Dr. Krishna Kodey: Revolutionizing Human Connection Through AI-Powered Digital PresenceDr. Krishna Kodey Drug Safety Specialist, entrepreneur, and innovator, is redefining the role of artificial intelligence in addressing one of society’s most pressing challenges,loneliness. Inspired by the loss of his grandparents at a young age and years of caring for elderly patients in Toronto, Canada, he envisioned a technology that could preserve human connection beyond physical absence. His answer is VLA(Virtual Loved Assist, Series-1) From iAVATARS, a Michigan-based startup set to launch in late 2026. Powered by the proprietary iSoul 1.0 platform, iAVATARS enables meaningful conversations with lifelike digital representations of loved ones, preserving their voice, personality, and memories. More than a chat-bot or application, it offers an emotional presence within the home. With patents pending across three major countries, Dr. Kodey’s innovation is pioneering a new era of AI-driven human companionship and emotional well being.Dr. Anirudh Gupta: Redefining Education Through Visionary LeadershipDr. Anirudh Gupta is India’s most influential Edupreneur, Philanthropist, Author, Angel Investor & Social Impact Leader whose contributions have reshaped modern education. As CEO of DCM Group of Schools, established in 1946, he leads innovation, technology integration and experiential learning, positioning DCM as a benchmark for future-ready education. A globally recognized thought leader, Dr. Gupta has represented India at  international eduforums, sharing insights on innovation, leadership, emerging technologies and the future of learning. He holds leadership roles in  NISA, ISFI, CII and PHD Chamber of Commerce. Recipient of several national and international honours, including Education Icon of the Year consecutively, he continues to drive educational transformation, youth empowerment and social impact by building avant-garde institutions, shaping the future of education.Dr. Vedala Srinivas (Hon): A Visionary Leader in Spirituality, Business, Cinema and Social Service Dr. Vedala Srinivas (Hon) is a distinguished Clairvoyant, Film Producer, Merchant Exporter and Philanthropist whose remarkable journey reflects excellence across Spirituality, Business, Creative Arts and Social Service. Widely respected for his intuitive guidance, visionary leadership and commitment to uplifting society, he has positively influenced individuals and professionals both in India and internationally. As the Founder of Sri Mandiram Productions and Srimandiram Traders and Services, he has successfully blended entrepreneurship with innovation, quality, integrity and ethical business practices.Beyond his professional accomplishments, he actively supports community welfare, cultural preservation, humanitarian initiatives and educational empowerment. Driven by a strong sense of purpose, service and responsibility Dr. Vedala Srinivas (Hon) continues to inspire positive transformation through his dedication to personal growth, social development,spiritual values and nation-building efforts.www.vedalasrinivas.com    www.srimandiramexports.comMr. Umesh Malik: Driving Excellence Through Strategic Procurement LeadershipUmesh Malik is a distinguished procurement leader and currently serves as Vice President with one of India’s largest integrated energy and materials organizations. With over three decades of experience, he has been instrumental in transforming procurement into a strategic driver of business value and organizational growth. A Mechanical Engineering graduate with postgraduate qualifications in Business Administration, he combines technical expertise with visionary leadership. Passionate about nurturing future-ready professionals, he has championed competency-based learning, capability transformation, and leadership development throughout his career. Backed by globally recognized certifications and experience as a certified leadership assessor, Umesh Malik continues to inspire through his commitment to excellence, continuous learning, and impactful leadership.The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: NYT slams Microsoft for building copyright-infringing supercomputer for OpenAI
description: NYT shifts OpenAI/Microsoft copyright claims after SCOTUS ruling against Sony.
content:
In a heavily redacted court filing Thursday, The New York Times proposed to amend its copyright complaint against OpenAI and Microsoft to clarify a claim and allege that Microsoft actively encouraged OpenAI to steal NYT works by building a bespoke supercomputing system ranked among the most powerful in the world.
NYT's motion comes after the Supreme Court sided with Cox Communications in a case where Sony tried and failed to claim that Cox was contributing to music piracy as an Internet service provider, which set a new standard for contributory infringement. Moving forward, plaintiffs will have to prove that parties intentionally acted to induce illegal conduct. Recognizing that the legal precedent has changed, the NYT now wants to amend its complaint to align its contributory infringement claim against Microsoft with that new standard.
“Today, we asked the court for permission to file an amended complaint that further strengthens our case, clarifying our claim of contributory infringement against Microsoft based on new law and new evidence uncovered during discovery,” Graham James, an NYT spokesperson, said in a statement provided to Ars.Read full article

title: Nehal Mota champions a holistic approach to financial fitness through Finnovate
description: As conversations around personal finance increasingly move beyond investments and returns, a growing number of financial professionals are advocating for a more comprehensive approach to managing money. Among them is Nehal Mota, Co-Founder of Finnovate, who has spent nearly two decades promoting financial fitness as a long-term discipline rather than a short-term pursuit of wealth creation.Founded in 2007 by Nehal Mota and Naveen Singh, Finnovate was established with the objective of helping individuals and families make informed financial decisions through structured planning. Over the years, the firm has worked with thousands of families across India, focusing on goal-based financial strategies designed to align with different life stages and aspirations.Mota's approach stems from the belief that financial well-being cannot be measured solely through investment performance. Instead, she advocates a broader framework that encompasses multiple aspects of personal finance, including cash flow management, risk protection, wealth creation, retirement preparedness, estate planning, and financial goal setting.This philosophy led to the development of a financial fitness model that seeks to bring clarity and discipline to the way people manage their finances. Rather than emphasizing products, the model focuses on creating long-term financial habits and decision-making frameworks that can help individuals navigate changing economic circumstances.Under Mota's leadership, Finnovate adopted a hybrid advisory structure that combines technology with human expertise. The model aims to provide personalized financial guidance that is structured and purposeful, while maintaining transparency and consistency throughout the planning process.According to Mota, financial planning should remain unbiased and aligned with the interests of clients. “My main objective with Finnovate was to build a fiduciary advisory model that remains unbiased, product-agnostic, and deeply personalized. We believe financial planning should empower families to make confident decisions and unlock their true financial potential,” she said.As financial literacy continues to gain prominence across India, Mota remains focused on encouraging individuals to view financial planning as an ongoing journey rather than a one-time activity. Her work reflects a growing shift within the industry toward holistic financial wellness, where long-term security and life goals take precedence over short-term market outcomes.The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: FCC accused of hiding Chairman Carr's messages with DOGE and Musk
description: FCC refuses to provide messages, has "wasted a year" of court's time, filing says.
content:
An advocacy group trying to investigate DOGE's influence on the Federal Communications Commission accused the FCC of failing to comply with a public records request and of concealing Chairman Brendan Carr's use of the Signal messaging service.
"The evidence clearly demonstrates that the FCC has acted in bad faith by withholding documents responsive to Plaintiffs’ FOIA [Freedom of Information Act] request," journalist Nina Burleigh and advocacy group Frequency Forward said in a filing yesterday in US District Court for the District of Columbia. "The FCC acted in bad faith when it redefined the search criteria without notice to Plaintiffs or this Court. Further, the FCC acted in bad faith by concealing the fact that the Chairman Carr has a Signal account on a phone he uses to conduct government business."
Burleigh and Frequency Forward sued the FCC last year, alleging that it violated the Freedom of Information Act by wrongfully withholding agency records. In August 2025, a federal judge ordered the FCC to produce documents and criticized it for a “vague and uninformative” response to the lawsuit.Read full article

title: Netflix now requires every user profile to be tied to unique email address
description: Update began June 15 and will no longer allow you to share your login info.
content:
Recently, my father called me in a panic.
There were just a few minutes until Netflix would start streaming a live MMA event, and he couldn’t get into my account. For a while, he had accessed Netflix as an add-on member with his own profile through my household’s account. That day, however, he was logged out and couldn’t use my login credentials to watch Netflix. Instead, he saw a prompt asking him to “add an email address to your profile” to continue.

      A Reddit user shared this image of the notification that affected profile owners are seeing.
        Credit:
          Scotti_Dev/Reddit/Netflix
      
After some frantic phone troubleshooting and a couple of password resets, we realized that my father had to create his own login to continue using the extra profile I paid for. Although I was able to get him set up in time (for some disappointing bouts), the situation was confusing and inconvenient.Read full article

title: Antibiotic "megacluster" discovery provides new strategy to fight superbugs
description: It's "an exciting advance in efforts to restock the antibiotic arsenal."
content:
Antibiotic resistance has loomed over humans since the moment we started using antibiotics. In the 20th century, the drugs downgraded potentially life-threatening bacterial infections to mere inconveniences—a miracle of modern medicine, it seemed. But the drugs aren't really a human invention; we mostly swiped them from microbes, which have been locked in an arms race with each other for centuries. Microbial evolution has crafted both deadly molecules and clever tricks to dodge death as the wee organisms endlessly battle over turf and resources. More than 80 percent of the antibiotics used in clinics today are based on those turf-war weapons, which scientists refer to as "natural products."
For decades, humans mined antibiotic molecules from microbes and tweaked them to develop new drugs, staying ahead of evolution's cunning countermeasures. But in recent times, new natural products have been harder to find, and the pipeline of new antibiotics has slowed to a trickle. Meanwhile, existing antibiotics have been overused, and resistance has mounted to critical levels. Most antibiotics are single bioactive molecules, and some can be thwarted with single mutations. While the current situation is dire, a study in Nature this week reports a compelling discovery that not only points to a potentially new antibiotic regimen, but also an entirely new strategy to once again get ahead in the microbial arms race.
Exciting find
The study, led by biomedical researcher Eric Brown at McMaster University in Ontario, Canada, reports the discovery of a large block of genes—dubbed a "megacluster"—that codes for four molecules that appear to work in concert to derail a single essential metabolic pathway.Read full article

title: The next-generation leaders reshaping business
description: New Delhi [India], June 22: A new wave of entrepreneurs, founders, and executives is transforming industries through innovation, strategic vision, and bold leadership. These next-generation leaders are not only driving business growth but also creating meaningful impact by challenging conventions and embracing change. In this special feature, we spotlight emerging changemakers whose ideas, resilience, and forward-thinking approach are shaping the future of business and inspiring the next era of leadership.Richa Gangani, Founder, Richa’s Fitness LabRicha Gangani, Founder of Richa’s Fitness Lab, is dedicated to helping individuals build healthier lives through practical, personalized wellness solutions. Her approach is rooted in the belief that sustainable health comes from understanding each person’s unique lifestyle, habits, and nutritional needs rather than following one-size-fits-all trends. Through her work, she empowers clients to adopt realistic routines that fit seamlessly into their daily lives, making long-term consistency achievable. Richa advocates a balanced approach to fitness and nutrition, focusing on lasting well-being over quick fixes. By simplifying health and encouraging mindful lifestyle choices, she continues to inspire individuals to achieve meaningful and sustainable transformations.Mr. Chakravarthi Mittapalli, Vice Chairman, Narasaraopeta Engineering CollegeFor over two decades, Narasaraopeta Engineering College (NEC) has shaped technical education in Andhra Pradesh through academic excellence, industry collaboration, innovation and career-focused learning. With a 48-acre campus, autonomous status, NAAC A+ accreditation and NIRF 201-300 ranking band, NEC is advancing AI-led engineering education under Vice Chairman Chakravarthi Mittapalli, a technologist, entrepreneur and education visionary. The institution’s AI & Robotics Centres of Innovation, industry skilling programmes, incubation initiatives, Intel-backed AI Lab, certified training, internships, research exposure, next-generation tools and Microsoft AI Odyssey recognition reflect its focus on building a future-ready youth.Mr. Tejas Chavan, Director, Green SpacesMr. Tejas Chavan is the Director of Green Spaces and a purpose-driven entrepreneur committed to advancing sustainable living and eco-conscious hospitality. Under his leadership, Grape County Eco Resort & Spa has emerged as one of Maharashtra’s leading eco-tourism destinations, guided by the philosophy of “Co-Exist with Nature.” He has spearheaded transformative initiatives including the plantation of over 60,000 native trees, creation of five rainwater-harvested lakes, 100% water recycling systems, and biodiversity restoration projects. A passionate advocate for sustainable development, regenerative farming, rural empowerment, and responsible tourism, Mr. Chavan continues to demonstrate how environmental conservation and economic growth can work together to create lasting value for communities and future generations.Rituu Jai Mehhta is Director – Marketing (South) & Centre Director, Phoenix Mall of AsiaRituu Jai Mehhta is Director – Marketing (South) & Centre Director, Phoenix Mall of Asia , where she leads marketing and destination strategy across more than five million square feet of premium retail developments in Bengaluru, Chennai, and Pune. With over two decades of experience in retail, marketing, and consumer engagement, she has been instrumental in advancing experiential retail by integrating fashion, entertainment, beauty, gastronomy, and community-led experiences into destination ecosystems. Recognized for combining strategic business leadership with innovative marketing, Rituu has received multiple industry honours, including The Times of India's Woman Power Award as well as Karnataka Woman Entrepreneurship Award among others for her contributions to retail and destination marketing.Mr. Karteesh Reddy Madgula, Mr. Lakshmi Narayana G., and Mr. Sharat Ventrapragada, Designated Partners of GHR Lakshmi Urbanblocks Infra LLPDeveloped by Mr. Karteesh Reddy Madgula, Mr. Lakshmi Narayana G., and Mr. Sharat Ventrapragada (Partners of GHR Lakshmi Urbanblocks Infra LLP), The Cascades Neopolis is a landmark ultra-luxury residential project in Kokapet, Hyderabad. Spanning 7.34 acres, the development features five 63-storey towers housing 1,189 residences and 10 triplex penthouses.As India’s first and the world’s largest WELL pre-certified residential project, this international collaboration prioritizes wellness-led, future-ready living. The joint venture solidified its regional realty leadership by securing Neopolis Plot 15, a premium 4.03-acre corner land parcel boasting dual 150-foot road frontages, with a record-setting highest bid of ₹151.25 crore per acre, ensuring immense long-term value.Mr. Rishi Bharadwaj, CEO and Mr. Krishna Pradyumna, CTO of CultNerdsIT SolutionsCultNerds, Co-founded by Mr. Rishi Bharadwaj, CEO and Mr. Krishna Pradyumna, CTO in 2021 and headquartered in Hyderabad, CultNerds IT Solutions is an award-winning digital transformation agency serving 50+ clients across real estate, hospitality, luxury clubs, retail and education. With 65+ professionals, the company blends technology, marketing, data and AI to improve customer acquisition, engagement and business performance. Its in-house products support data analytics, enrichment, customer nurturing, automation and intelligent workflows. Rishi drives a framework-led culture focused on creativity, accountability and outcomes, while Krishna leads technical innovation and scalable product development for thousands of users.Jimmy Mistry, Founder & Chairman, Della TownshipsDesign Futurist, Jimmy Mistry founded Della Townships, one of India’s fastest-growing asset-light real estate integrated township platforms, to redefine how India lives, works, and dreams through design-led, future-ready, theme-based lifestyle cities. From a 30-key glamping resort in Lonavala to a ₹46,480 crore township pipeline spanning 4,288 acres across India, Jimmy Mistry believes "We become the new Uber or the new Airbnb of the real estate industry. We are an asset-light real estate model." Removing the two biggest barriers to real estate expansion, Della's asset-light model eliminates the need for land ownership and direct regulatory approvals. Instead, the company partners with landowners and developers, focusing on what it does best—conceptualisation, design, development, marketing, and operations through its proprietary CDDMO model.Piyush Jhunjhunwala, Founder & CEO, StockifyPiyush Jhunjhunwala is a seasoned finance professional, Chartered Accountant (India), and Certified Public Accountant (USA) with over 20 years of experience across global markets. Before founding Stockify, he held senior leadership roles at PepsiCo, Reckitt, and Coty, building expertise in finance, strategy, governance, and business operations across India and international markets. As Founder and CEO, Piyush has led Stockify’s mission to democratize access to India’s pre-IPO and unlisted shares market. Under his leadership, the company has expanded into alternative investments with the launch of its Alternative Investment Fund (AIF), evolving into a comprehensive WealthTech platform. He is now focused on broadening investor access to global private market opportunities, including U.S. private equity investments, helping drive long-term wealth creation and portfolio diversification.Dr. Eadhu Krishna K, Managing Director & Chief Consultant, SKCCR Care, Ernakulam, Kerala.Dr. Eadhu Krishna K is a healthcare entrepreneur and the Managing Director & Chief Consultant of SKCCR Care – Ernakulam, Kerala. Building on the institution’s 46-year legacy, he began training under his father at 18 and has since accumulated over 14 years of clinical research and experience in integrative cancer care. He has treated thousands of patients through evidence-based homoeopathic protocols and was honored with the Leading Innovator in Homoeopathic Cancer Care award by the Health Minister of Kerala. At 25, he founded Dr.BOZ Pharma, a company developing plant-based nutraceuticals and homoeopathic formulations for chronic lifestyle diseases, combining clinical expertise with pharmaceutical innovation.Jesicaa Siingh, Founder & CEO, Stanley CommunicationsJesicaa Siingh is the Founder & CEO of Stanley Communications, a global PR agency specializing in luxury, fashion, beauty, hospitality, and culture. As an entrepreneur, she has built a 135+ member women-led team while expanding the agency’s presence to Dubai. Driven by a passion for storytelling and brand building, she has helped shape the India narratives of global brands including Valentino, Bvlgari, Messika, Amiri, Dyson, Versace, SK-II, and Galeries Lafayette.A champion of women in leadership, Jesicaa has built a culture of creativity and accountability, earning recognitions including Times 40 Under 40 and TV9 Network SHE Star Honours.Together, these next-generation leaders represent the evolving face of business—bold, innovative, and impact-driven. Through their vision, adaptability, and determination, they are not only transforming their respective industries but also setting the benchmark for the future of leadership and enterprise.The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: MBA faces crisis in AI era, demands urgent rethink from business schools
description: For a degree that teaches disruption, it is quite remarkable how little the traditional MBA model has changed in the 118 years since its inception at Harvard. In fact, one could argue that the MBA has done a remarkable job of avoiding disruption. Over these years, there have probably been only five major innovations—the introduction of the case method at Harvard in 1924, the launch of the executive MBA at Chicago Booth in 1943, the advent of the quantitative MBA in the 1950s, the birth of the one-year MBA at INSEAD in 1959, and the launch of the stackable online MBA in the 2010s.Apart from these, the MBA has remained remarkably resilient, seemingly immune to changes in the world around it. It has endured the dot-com bust, the global meltdown in 2008, and the global pandemic. At various points, commentators have sounded the death knell for the MBA, convinced that it is losing its sheen and value. Yet, it continues with a product that supporters argue has stood the test of time, while detractors dismiss it as being out of touch with reality.Today, we are once again at a crossroads as we confront AI and its impact on jobs. If I were to pose the question in the title of this essay to an MBA class, I would expect about 20% to say “Yes”, about 30% to say “No”, and 50% to sit on the fence with a typical “It depends” or “Maybe”. This essay is aimed at these “Maybes”. I argue that over the last 100-plus years, the MBA has survived change but has barely evolved. The time is ripe for a radical rethink.In India, the IIMs were founded in the 1960s, driven by the urge to create world-class management institutions. IIM Ahmedabad partnered with Harvard, and IIM Calcutta with MIT Sloan. The aim was to move away from the rigidities of the traditional university system and enable these institutions to pursue high-quality research and pedagogy.Sixty-plus years later, one could argue that while we have got the pedagogy right, we continue to lag behind global b-schools in creating new knowledge. This may have been acceptable when changes in the global environment were slow or predictable. That is no longer the case. AI has transformed the career landscape, accelerating the emergence of non-linear pathways.Also Read: Why MBA education in the age of AI must move beyond analysis for leadershipDisruptions abound—driven by entrepreneurs who challenge the status quo, question traditional business models, and take on well-established incumbents. India is at the forefront of these disruptions. Shouldn’t we be driving more research in this area? Shouldn’t we be creating new knowledge and disseminating it in the classroom? In fact, shouldn’t we be practicing what we teach and disrupting the way MBAs are designed and structured?Instead, the system has spawned innumerable PGDM and MBA programmes, most of them virtually identical except for placement outcomes, campus infrastructure, and brand name. We are becoming victims of the very rigidity we once sought to escape.To be sure, there is innovation at the margins. Most b-schools today include AI and analytics as core components of their curriculum. Courses on e-commerce, digital marketing, and data science are widely offered to ensure that graduates are tech-literate. Several institutions are also experimenting with pedagogical approaches such as simulations, flipped classrooms, and role-plays.Yet, the core architecture remains largely unchanged. There have been few attempts to challenge the traditional two-year format aimed at students with limited work experience. Even fewer have questioned the functional silos in which students specialise—finance, marketing, operations, OB/HR, and others. Governed by regulations and rankings, incentives are aligned towards consistency and incremental change.This creates a widening gap between what recruiters need from fresh graduates and what institutions are able to deliver.Also Read: AI readiness in Indian business schools beyond the signallingAs institutions around the world grapple with a commoditised offering, value-conscious students, and increasingly sceptical recruiters, there is a clear need for a more agile MBA—one that not only adapts to changing market needs but anticipates them. Institutions must adopt a growth mindset: challenging long-held assumptions, listening closely to recruiters and students, experimenting even at the risk of failure, and executing at a far greater pace and scale. AI presents an opportunity to make this shift.The widespread adoption of AI across organisations will require graduates who are skilled in using these tools. However, the ability to simply use AI is unlikely to create significant value. A study in the US by neuroscientist and entrepreneur Vivienne Ming found that students performed poorly on long-term prediction tasks without AI, with any LLM outperforming them.More strikingly, when students were allowed to use an LLM, over 90% simply copied responses, performing no better than the tool itself. However, about 10% demonstrated superior outcomes by thinking critically and engaging with the output.It is this ability—to think, reason, and solve ill-defined problems—that will endure. The market is ready for an agile MBA that challenges the status quo and builds career-ready skills for a future that will be increasingly shaped by AI.

title: Ars Live: What's the latest in the aftermath of the New Glenn catastrophe?
description: Join us on the livestream at 1 pm ET and ask questions about the aftermath of New Glenn.
content:
Nearly a month has passed since the New Glenn rocket exploded on its launch pad in Florida, creating a massive fireball. It was likely the largest ever rocket explosion at the historic Florida spaceport, and we are still dealing with its implications today.
The rocket's explosion took out its only launch pad, LC-36A. So even if Blue Origin can quickly diagnose the cause of the failure, it has nowhere to launch the New Glenn rocket from. Company officials, including founder Jeff Bezos, have said the vehicle will return to flight at LC-36A before the end of this year, though there is widespread skepticism about that timeline.
Meanwhile, we have more questions than answers about a rocket that had become increasingly central to the needs of NASA and commercial customers. What does this failure mean for the Artemis Program to land humans on the Moon? What do we know about the timing of Artemis III and the lunar landing mission, Artemis IV? What about the Moon base?Read full article

title: VW may close four factories to adapt to the future, report says
description: With falling sales in the US and especially China, VW Group wants to restructure.
content:
Volkswagen Group is considering what was previously unthinkable: closing up to four factories in Germany and instituting layoffs that would shrink the workforce by 15 percent.
2025 was a bad year for Europe's largest automaker. Its sales were essentially flat, but profits were anything but, dropping 44 percent to just 6.9 billion euros ($7.9 billion) as operating margins more than halved. The red ink looks set to continue bleeding through 2026, and in March, the company announced it would cut 50,000 jobs in Germany by 2030 as part of a plan to adapt. Now, according to a report in Manager Magazin, those job losses may double.
The automaker did well selling EVs in Europe last year, but sales in North America and China fell and continue to fall, and tariffs have had a significant effect.Read full article

title: Feedbacks upon feedbacks: Rock weathering and the climate
description: Rock weathering may release or draw down carbon dioxide—it depends on the rock.
content:
Since the early 1980s, Earth scientists have understood that erosion and weathering of rock slowly removes CO2 from the atmosphere, regulating Earth’s climate on geological timescales. But recent studies have shown that erosion can also emit CO2 by oxidizing organic carbon contained in eroding sediments. It hasn’t been clear how this competition between removal by rock weathering and emission by organic carbon weathering ends up affecting Earth’s climate.
A new study in the journal Nature Communications uses the geological past to test how these competing effects added up. Doctor Madeleine Stow of the University of Oxford, with colleagues from across the UK and France, examined a volcanically triggered episode of global warming that happened in the early part of the Jurassic period, 183 million years ago, known as the “Toarcian Ocean Anoxic Event.”
They found that eroding organic carbon amplified climate warming at the time, suggesting that the same process may apply to modern climate change. But the extent to which the past is prologue is uncertain.Read full article

title: Researchers discover why fructose doesn't satisfy hunger like glucose
description: A new study found that fructose and glucose may look the same on a nutrition label, but the brain treats them very differently. In mice, glucose strongly reduced activity in hunger-promoting brain cells, while fructose had a much weaker effect. High-fructose corn syrup triggered a stronger response and was preferred by the animals. The findings suggest that the type of sugar—not just the calories—can influence appetite and food preferences.

title: Mallika Sagar straddles the worlds of art and sports auctions with equal ease
description: The first time Mallika Sagar conducted an auction was for Christie’s in New York, selling baseball memorabilia. She was just 25, and her trainer had warned her: “This is a room full of American men used to seeing American men [as auctioneers]. When they see you, a young Indian woman, just be ready, because I don’t think they are going to be ready,” recalls Sagar.The art history graduate knew little about baseball, but says the auction went off pretty well. In hindsight, it was prophetic, because Sagar, who specialises in Indian art and is an auctioneer with Pundole’s art gallery in Mumbai, has since broken ground as the first female sports auctioneer in India.She debuted in an Indian sports auction in 2021, as a reserve to Briton Hugh Edmeades, who had been conducting Indian Premier League (IPL) auctions since 2019. “It was the year of Covid, he needed a back-up in case he tested positive after landing in India, and reached out to me. Luckily, I didn’t need to step in as he was fine, but that year I was asked to do the Pro Kabaddi League (PKL),” says Sagar, recalling her route into sports auctioneering.In February 2023, she was called upon to conduct the auction for the inaugural season of the Women’s Premier League (WPL) and, end of that year, she took up her first IPL assignment—the mini-auction in Dubai. Last year, she conducted the Rugby Premier League auction on the request of actor Rahul Bose, the president of Rugby India and “an old friend”. This April, she hosted the auction for Season 2, where the women’s tournament was launched. “I am now down four auctions for WPL, three for IPL, five for kabaddi and two for rugby,” she says.Given her relatively recent, high-profile exposure as a sports auctioneer, it might be easy to overlook her training and experience with art. Her stint at Christie’s during her college years in the US gave her the chance to go through different departments within the company and gain an in-depth understanding of how an auction house works.Also Read: Ishita Moitra: Bringing contemporary female gaze into mainstream narrativesAfter her graduation, Christie’s called her back, but this time to work for them in New York, when they were moving offices from Park Avenue to the Rockefeller Center. “It was fantastic, I understood exactly what is needed at the back-end of an auction house,” she recalls.What also followed was formal training in the art and science of conducting live auctions. “There was an auctioneer training programme, which I attended every Friday. There was a trainer and a theatre coach, because you have to learn how to breathe and how to speak. And it’s all a bit of theatre and drama, because you’re up there for a few hours. It’s about not being nervous, the math, the numbers, how to balance different things,” she says.Back in India by end-2000, Sagar was doing between three and six charity art auctions a year, which were popular at the time as a way to raise donations for NGOs. It was in 2011 that she came back full-time to art, under Pundole’s umbrella, starting with two auctions a year and now conducting close to 10.As an auctioneer, Sagar explains, you should be able to sell any kind of art. “If you are in one of the larger auction houses internationally, you could be selling furniture one day, silver one day, art one day. I am lucky I’m a specialist today in the area that I also sell in—modern Indian contemporary paintings.”Auctioneers have the insights to know which paintings are going to do well, which are the rare ones. But although Sagar makes it sound like a cakewalk—”It’s a system: You have an estimate from the auctioneer’s book, you have a list of lot numbers, we go in the order of our catalogues, and you have the artist’s name, the title of the artwork, the estimate, the result”—reality can often throw curveballs. An auctioneer has to constantly read the room, keep the audience engaged and enthused, while encouraging the in-house team of experts handling bids coming through phone calls, the app and website, all while keeping an eye on the clock. W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026But how different is it to auction artworks and sportspeople? “Anything to do with art or memorabilia, anything that’s not a human being essentially, is driven by passion,” says Sagar. “So, people spend maybe more than what the object may be worth on paper, sometimes a lot more and you have to run with that.”Art auctions can also be casual, given the familiarity she has with the audience and dealers. But sports auctions are a different ballgame. “I’m a lot more measured at a sports auction because the teams are serious and so, I’m not laughing and joking. It’s live on TV and with 10 million viewers, all of whom have an opinion. When you put yourself out in a public space with cricket, you’re really asking for trouble,” she now laughs. “I don’t have a thick skin. And when it first happened [social media trolling], I was in tears.” Sagar does clarify, though, that the criticism is perhaps not gendered, and maybe a male auctioneer would also face the same nitpicking.“Mallika is very professional and knows the business. She has a cool temperament and everything that an auctioneer needs. And she does prepare hard,” says Charu Sharma, TV presenter and founder of PKL, who is an auctioneer himself. “She has made a big difference in this field in India… you’ve got people here who are just as professional, just as good. I hope she is able to attract other women to also become auctioneers or become involved in sport with her calm, poise, professionalism, and not just the fact that there’s a lady there.”

title: SpaceX plans to launch Starlink mobile service in the US
description: Move would test whether group can turn ambition into a mass-market phone business.
content:
Elon Musk’s SpaceX has told investors that it plans to launch a new Starlink mobile service for US consumers, in a move that would upend the country’s multibillion-dollar phone network market.
The company’s president and chief operating officer, Gwynne Shotwell, told investors during a recent IPO roadshow that the group was considering launching a Starlink retail product and could build its own terrestrial US mobile network, according to four people familiar with the matter.
The move would require Starlink to build a new retail offering by selling mobile contracts to individual customers, competing directly with the three big US network operators Verizon Wireless, AT&T. and T-Mobile.Read full article

title: Ishita Moitra: Bringing contemporary female gaze into mainstream narratives
description: There is a scene in almost every Ishita Moitra story where people take time to reveal who they really are. A woman says something honest over drinks. A family argument suddenly turns tender. A joke hides a heartbreak. A glamorous world exposes its hidden fragments.Across television, OTT and cinema, Moitra’s writing stands on these human contradictions. Her women are not perfect, rarely symbolic, and almost never written to fit stereotypes. They are ambitious and nurturing, insecure and self-aware, funny and vulnerable, often within the same scene.That sense of storyteller has made Moitra one of the most distinctive writing voices in the film industry. From Four More Shots Please! and The Test Case to Rocky Aur Rani Kii Prem Kahaani, her storytelling has consistently brought a contemporary female gaze into mainstream narratives without losing its spark.“The idea is to put forth real women and not the Madonna-whore complex,” Moitra says. “Women can be everything. One day you can actually be sacrificial, and the other day you can be ambitious, because that’s what real women are.”W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026For filmmaker Karan Johar, who collaborated with Moitra on Rocky Aur Rani Kii Prem Kahaani (2023), her creative energy is unmistakable. “Ishita for me is a bona fide force of nature. She has an incredible sense of humour. She can bring pathos and humour to perfection… she creates a certain kind of magic in her writing, which I find very rare,” he tells Forbes India.Long before awards, film premieres and streaming success, Moitra discovered her writing skills through an essay competition in school. “During an essay competition in my 8th or 9th standard, the teacher was thrilled with what I wrote,” she recalls. “She read it out in front of the class. I realised that I love it [writing] and somehow it resonates with other people too.”That moment stayed with her. At a time when most career choices revolved around engineering or medicine, Moitra studied journalism at Delhi University before pursuing her master’s in mass communication at Jamia Millia Islamia. It was during those years that she realised fiction—and specifically screenwriting—was where she truly belonged.“That’s when I realised that what I wanted to do was write fiction… not novels, but writing for the big screen,” she says. “It was a journey that started at 13 and crystallised around 21.”Today, Moitra belongs to a generation of writers changing how women are written in Indian entertainment. Instead of presenting them through ideas of idealism, her characters exist in shades of grey, ambitious, insecure, loving, selfish, emotional and funny at once.That perspective has made her work resonate with the audiences, especially women who see themselves reflected in her characters. Moitra says much of her writing comes from observing people around her—friends, mothers, family conversations and everyday emotions. “I love going to family gatherings and hearing people talk,” she says. “Family gatherings are like gold mines for writers.”Along with her husband, filmmaker Neeraj Udhwani, Moitra co-created and co-produced Single Papa, a show exploring parenting and masculinity through a single father’s journey. This also marked a shift for her from being just a writer to co-creation and production. “When you are a writer otherwise, it’s like you’re just a teacher correcting papers,” she says with her characteristic humour. “Now [as a producer] I am the mother who is also the teacher.”Johar believes that emotional intelligence and shared cultural memory are central to Moitra’s voice as a writer. “I felt like we immediately connected as people, as friends and as collaborators,” he says. “Both of us have the same references from Hindi cinema. Both of us know the words of every single song that exists in the musicscape of Indian cinema.”Also Read: From small parts to centre stage, the rise of Chhaya KadamThat love for Hindi cinema runs deep for Moitra. “I’ve grown up watching masala Hindi films, and I love them. That is the film nutrition that I have been fed.”However, Moitra rejects the idea of writing differently for “mass” audiences. “I try to be authentic to the world of that story,” she says. “Sometimes when we start writing thinking what people expect from us, you can get a little muddled.”Motherhood has become one of the strongest anchors to shape Moitra’s perspective, changing the way she thinks about her work and legacy. “Whenever I do anything now, I think: Will my daughter like this? Is this something I want her to see and say, ‘My mama wrote this?’” she says. That grounding, she believes, becomes especially important in an industry dependent on constant validation. Family gives her perspective during both success and uncertainty.“Through my writing, I want to leave people with a smile on their faces and gratitude for relationships,” she says.

title: Microsoft Extends Windows 10 Extended Security Updates for Another Year Despite Pushing Users to Update to Windows 11
description: Microsoft continues to recommend migrating to Windows 11
content: Microsoft's consumer ESU programme for Windows 10 users was set to provide security updates until October this year. However, Microsoft has now quietly extended the programme by another year. As a result, eligible Windows 10 users will continue to receive security updates through October 12, 2027. An existing ESU license can be used to cover up to 10 devices.  Devices running on Windows 10, version 22H2 Home, Professional, Pro Education or Workstations edition are eligible to join the consumer Windows 10 ESU programme.

title: Video | Is the Skoda Enyaq vRS the ultimate one-car garage?
description:
Looking for the perfect blend of weekday skills and weekend thrills? This electric family SUV could be the answer

The perfect two-car garage. It’s a fantasy motoring enthusiasts have debated for decades.
Not familiar with the concept? It’s simple: if you had to choose two cars – one for everyday duties like commuting, the school run and shopping trips, and another purely for weekend B-road blasts – which would you choose? If you’re ever stuck for conversation with a fellow enthusiast, pose this question and watch the sparks fly.
But, alas, in today’s economic climate, the dream of running two cars – particularly when one is reserved almost entirely for Sundays – is becoming harder and harder to justify. And that means more and more drivers are starting to search for one car that’s capable of covering all bases: sensible when it needs to be, fun when it doesn’t.
Watch the video below as we explore whether the 335bhp all-electric Skoda Enyaq vRS really can deliver the best of both worlds.


Skoda Enyaq vRS: the electric family SUV that can do it all
There’s a particular genius to the vRS badge. Since the first Octavia vRS arrived in 2001, those three letters have stood for meaningful performance, real-world practicality and strong value. It’s the everyman performance car formula, refined over decades and now applied to Skoda’s electric family SUV flagship.
The Enyaq vRS continues that tradition with 335bhp, 697Nm and dual-motor all-wheel drive, delivering 0-62mph in 5.4 seconds. But it’s not just about straight-line pace. Dynamic Chassis Control, lowered suspension and revised steering calibration give it a level of composure and adjustability that no electric SUV this size has any right to, allowing it to switch between relaxed motorway cruiser and pulse-raising B-road blaster at the touch of a button.
Crucially, it remains an easy car to live with day to day. The 84kWh battery delivers up to 344 miles of range (WLTP), while 185kW DC charging enables a 10–80% top-up in around 26 minutes.
Inside, that signature vRS DNA injects sporty flair into an otherwise plush, minimalist cabin. There are vRS-specific sports seats, a bespoke sports steering wheel and contrast detailing. As you’d expect from a Škoda, equipment is generous, with a large 13in infotainment screen, augmented reality head-up display, heated seats and a 12-speaker CANTON sound system all included as standard. 
Practicality remains a core strength. A 585-litre boot, generous rear space and Skoda’s trademark ‘Smart, Spacious and Stylish’ touches – including an ice scraper in the tailgate and an umbrella in the door – ensure it can still deal with everything hectic family life throws at it without breaking a sweat. Maybe it’s time you try it for yourself.
Learn more about the Skoda Enyaq vRS

title: From small parts to centre stage, the rise of Chhaya Kadam
description: Chhaya Kadam finds it hard to erase 2001 from her memory. It was a year when she lost both her brother and father. “I felt as though my world had collapsed. I was confined to my home. I knew I should step out and carry on with life, but I was unable to get myself to do that,” says the actor who also underwent two knee surgeries after damaging a ligament the same year.Kadam, who had acted in a few plays in school, was not interested in taking to the stage in college. She had taken to kabaddi until her damaged knees meant she had to stop pursuing the sport. The tragedy in her family during the same period left her disillusioned and morose.It was around this time that she saw a newspaper advertisement about an acting workshop by theatre veteran Waman Kendre in Mumbai. Kadam believed it was a good opportunity to meet new people instead of spending time with relatives or friends, who, in all probability, would discuss the turmoil in her life. The course turned out to be a blessing for her.“I enjoyed the process… I got to learn a lot, and not just acting. It taught me to be independent and shaped me as a human. It made me understand that one does not become an actor merely by attending workshops or doing photo shoots. It erased all such assumptions. I realised I had to get better at many things—for instance, I didn’t read the newspapers, and I had language and diction issues because I hailed from Konkan. The workshop developed me in more ways than I could have imagined,” says Kadam, who has starred in acclaimed Marathi films like Sairat and Fandry, as well as recent noteworthy ones like the Hindi comedy-drama Laapataa Ladies, Sister Midnight and the Grand Prix (Cannes)-winning Malayalam film All We Imagine as Light.W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026Kendre, who saw her from close quarters, describes her as a hardworking and passionate artiste. “It was evident that Chhaya would stand out. She had a lot of potential and was serious about her work. She is an honest actor,” the former director of the National School of Drama tells Forbes India. He adds that she trained for two-and-a-half months for his directorial venture and her first play, Zulwa, and ended up doing around 250 shows. That laid the foundation for her as an actor.As Kadam fell in love with acting during the workshop, she decided to pursue it as a profession. It was not easy, though, to make inroads for someone who did not come from a film family. Kadam’s father was a mill worker from Kalina, Mumbai, and her mother was a housewife. She was the youngest among five siblings—two brothers and three sisters.When the acting breaks did not come through, she took up smaller assignments such as working as an assistant for a Hindi serial. The hunt for proper parts continued. In fact, when she got to know that Doordarshan was making a short film, she approached the makers for a role. The director told her that casting was done and that they needed an assistant instead. She agreed, thinking it would help her make connections. The film was being shot in Baramati, Maharashtra, and as luck would have it, the role Kadam was vying for fell into her lap. The lady who was supposed to play the part did not turn up the day before her scene was scheduled to be filmed. The team asked Kadam to fill in, and she faced the camera for the first time—for Phatacha Pani (Marathi).“I struggled a lot in those five years after the workshop… in fact, I got my first play [Zulwa] in 2006. My family did not support me… they knew nothing about this industry or my battles. But all the obstacles that came my way during that phase made me stronger,” says Kadam. “I did everything with utmost sincerity and dedication. Even if I got one scene in a film, I accepted that and did justice to the part. But the industry is such that when you get work, it does not mean you have arrived. You have to keep looking for newer opportunities. And it is difficult to get those… it can take many years.”It indeed took a while for Kadam to stamp her authority with her performances. Today, the Madgaon Express actor is known for bringing authenticity to the characters she essays and for her nuanced approach to her craft. “With acting, you literally give birth to a person—their walk, mannerisms, body language, backstory, even if it’s a fictional character,” she explains. “I am an observant person. I study people. After I read what has been offered to me, I work on the small details and decorate the character.”Also Read: W-Power 2026: Why Huma Qureshi Chooses Reinvention Over ComfortTo play Suman Akka in Sairat, Kadam learnt Telugu from the camera crew on set, while in the case of Fandry, she took lessons in Kaikadi. “It is not enough to learn the lines. You need to know the language to understand the emotion,” she insists.According to Kendre, Kadam had an inferiority complex because of her economic status and language shortcomings. But she overcame those mental blocks and discovered herself as an actor and a human being. “Such a journey is rare. She has marched ahead confidently and has not looked back. I expect her to do a lot more,” he says.Achievements like going to Cannes for All We Imagine as Light are the icing on the cake for Kadam. She does not read too much into it though, saying it is in the past. “I like to live in the moment. I don’t think too far ahead. I just have to do good work,” says the actor, who likes to try various delicacies, travel to her native place in Konkan and spend time with the people closest to her.Conscious of the fact that she has to keep opening new doors for herself, Kadam appears unperturbed even if all this ends one day. “I came here with an empty bag. I have no fear about the bag being empty again,” she says.

title: Polymarket Reports $2.9 Million Theft, Says Customers Will Be Refunded
description: Compromised third-party dependency triggered phishing attack on users
content: Prediction market platform Polymarket has confirmed that users affected by a phishing attack stemming from a compromised third-party vendor will be fully refunded. Blockchain analyst Specter estimated that attackers stole nearly $2.94 million worth of cryptocurrency from at least 11 wallets after a malicious script was injected into the platform's website. Polymarket said the affected dependency has been removed and described the incident as isolated.

title: Rocket Report: China may soon attempt booster landing; Rocket Lab does rapid response
description: Is SpaceX planning to end its Transporter program?
content:
Welcome to Edition 8.47 of the Rocket Report! We have now very nearly reached the midpoint of 2026, a year in which several new US rockets were advertised as potentially making their debuts. But now, we have to wonder whether any of them—Rocket Lab's Neutron, Stoke Space's Nova, Relativity Space's Terran R, and Astra's Rocket 4—will make it. I'd probably put the over/under at something like 0.5 of these launching. Please share your thoughts in the comments below!
As always, we welcome reader submissions, and if you don't want to miss an issue, please subscribe using the box below (the form will not appear on AMP-enabled versions of the site). Each report will include information on small-, medium-, and heavy-lift rockets as well as a quick look ahead at the next three launches on the calendar.

    
                        
                  
      

Rocket Lab executes rapid response mission. Last Friday Rocket Lab launched the Victus Haze mission just 16 hours and 42 minutes after receiving the US Space Force’s Notice to Launch, beating the previous record by more than 10 hours, the company said. The launch was scarcely announced in advance, Ars reports. The only public indication of an impending launch was the release of a warning for pilots and sailors to steer clear of the rocket’s flight path. Rocket Lab did not provide a livestream of the launch, as it does for most of its missions.Read full article

title: Next-generation Hyundai Elantra brings striking new look
description:
New Elantra adopts 'Art of Steel' design principles and comes with a choice of petrol or hybrid

Hyundai has revealed the next iteration of its Elantra saloon, which brings a dramatic new look.
Named the Avante in its home market of South Korea, it is the most extreme example of the brand’s new 'Art of Steel' design language yet, with a number of sharp lines and aggressive angles across its bodywork.
It is slightly larger than the rival Skoda Octavia, owing to a 30mm increase in its wheelbase compared with the previous-generation Elantra. The saloon has also grown 30mm wider and Hyundai said it has prioritised improving interior space. 
Inside, it has been designed to be “cozy and comfortable”, according to Hyundai. The dashboard and door cards’ designs cocoon around the seats, with customisable LED backlighting. 
As in the recently revealed Ioniq 3 hatchback, the driver’s display is set high on the dashboard. This is intended to keep important information in the driver’s line of sight, so they do not have to divert their attention away from the road to check their speed or remaining range. 
A large infotainment touchscreen, measuring either 12.9in or 14.6in, takes centre stage. It runs on Hyundai’s new Pleos operating system, which will allow new functionality such as installing navigation and music apps in the screen, rather than having to use smartphone mirroring. An array of switches and dials for the climate control system sits just below the main screen.

In South Korea, it will be available with two powertrains: a 2.0-litre petrol four-cylinder with 147bhp and a 1.6-litre hybrid with 155bhp. 
The hybrid also brings a new predictive regenerative braking system that reads the road ahead and automatically adjusts the strength of the regenerative effect, helping to keep the battery topped up.
In addition, it gets a ‘stay mode’ that allows the small traction battery’s reserves to power the climate controls and infotainment system while parked, in similar fashion to an EV with a larger battery.
South Korean deliveries of the new Elantra will begin in the coming weeks, although Hyundai has yet to confirm whether it will be sold in mainland Europe or the UK. The previous-generation car was not made available in those regions, despite having been tested there. However, Autocar understands that it has not been ruled out for the latest iteration, suggesting Hyundai may have had a change of heart.
It also remains to be seen whether the high-performance Elantra N will return – and whether it too could make it to Europe.

title: Callum Design reinvents Jaguar XJ220 for new era
description:
Ian Callum's design consultancy is poised to reveal its take on the legendary supercar

The eponymous consultancy of acclaimed car designer Ian Callum has reinvented the Jaguar XJ220. 
The new take on the XJ220 has yet to be revealed in full, but a side-profile image published by Callum Design showcases a dramatically different styling direction compared with the original. 
It is notably sharper around its haunches and there is a greater slope at its rear end. 
However, several cues remain intact, including the egg-shaped side windows and air ducts along its flanks.
Callum Design described the reimagined XJ220 as a “concept” and a “design study”, emphasising that there are no concrete plans to build a road-going car. But it added that the project “shows what’s possible” when working with the company, suggesting that a client with enough cash to spend could commission their own car.
The reworked XJ220 is expected to be revealed in full in the coming months. October marks the 35th anniversary of the production XJ220’s unveiling at the Tokyo motor show and that could provide Callum Design a fitting opportunity to celebrate the supercar.
Ian Callum, who was director of design at Jaguar from 1999 to 2019, has in recent years put his own spin on several historic cars. These include the Wood and Pickett Mini and the Aston Martin Vanquish – the latter of which he also originally designed. His consultancy has also been responsible for building a road-legal version of the Jaguar C-X75 concept, the ill-fated successor to the XJ220 and a car originally created while he was Jaguar's design chief.

title: Can video games help us better understand quantum mechanics?
description: The world of quantum video games is vast – there are hundreds that are either inspired by quantum mechanics or use quantum computers in their development. Columnist Karmela Padavic-Callaghan explores how these could change our understanding of quantum physics, or even help us make better devices

title: Renault 4 and 5 due extra power and range in 2027
description:
New ‘Gen 2 Evo’ motors made their debut with the Twingo but will be soon be rolled out in larger cars

The Renault 4 and 5 will be updated next year, bringing more efficient and more powerful motors.
The R5 arrived in 2024, and it was followed by the R4, its crossover twin, in early 2025. Both models are based on the AmpR Small platform and use Renault’s second-generation externally excited-synchronous (EESM) motors, which are available in two configurations. 
The R5 Urban Range pairs a 121bhp EESM with a 40kWh battery, while the Comfort Range gets a 148bhp motor and a 52kWh battery pack. In the UK, the larger R4 is only available with the Comfort Range set-up.
These powertrains enable the R5 to deliver a range of either 192 or 252 miles. The latter figure is reduced to 250 miles in the chunkier R4.

Renault is now ready to roll out updated ‘Gen 2 Evo’ motors for the two models, drawing on its learnings from developing third-generation drive units for the next Scenic and Megane that are due in 2028.
“We are still working on how to improve the efficiency and power [of the Gen 2 motors],” said Marianne Bataillon, Renault’s director of EV motor and battery development. “We are going to apply these [changes] at the end of 2026 or beginning of 2027.”
Bataillon highlighted the motor’s inverter and reducers as points of improvement that have netting greater efficiency and improved power outputs.
The new motors will help to keep the R4 and R5 on a par with newer rivals on technical terms. The incoming Volkswagen ID Polo outdoes the R5 in the range stakes, offering either 204 or 283 miles between charges, depending on which powertrain it is fitted with. The same is true of the new Skoda Epiq: it yields either 190 or 272 miles of range, with the higher figure beating the R4.
Renault’s new Gen 2 Evo motor made its debut in the Twingo, which arrives in the UK at the end of this year. In that car, however, its output is reduced to 80bhp, owing to its city-focused remit.

title: Weather tracker: Heatwave expected to bring Germany and Poland to 40C
description: Austrian Grand Prix declares heat hazard as dramatic rise in temperatures forecast in central and northern EuropeTemperatures are forecast to rise dramatically in parts of central and northern Europe this weekend as the intense heatwave continues. In Germany and Poland, highs up to or exceeding 40C (104F) are expected on both Saturday and Sunday, days after swathes of France experienced similar extreme temperatures. The Austrian Grand Prix, taking place this weekend in Spielberg, has declared a heat hazard, the first race to do so this season. Temperatures are expected to rise into the low 30s celsius during the race, almost 10C warmer than usual for the venue.Across Europe, the intense heat has led to severe thunderstorms. In the Sverdlovsk region of central Russia, a strong tornado was reported on the evening of 22 June. Rated a 3 out of 5 on the international Fujita scale, the twister injured 16 people and destroyed or damaged about 100 homes and businesses in the town of Kushva. Emergency services have been working on restoring power to the town. Tornadoes are not unheard of in Russia but are rare. Forecasters say they may become more common in future as the climate breaks down. Storm warnings remained in effect in the southern part of the country on Friday. Continue reading...
